title: Riemann Hypothesis attack round 2 — numerical 4-part scan (orthogonal to R1 ladder)

angle_shift_from_r1.r1_style: analytical — Theorem B arithmetic -> Montgomery pair-correlation ladder -> conditional prime gap. result_lines=0 (zero numerics produced).
angle_shift_from_r1.r2_style: computational — probe 50 Odlyzko zeros via four independent RH-adjacent indicators. Produces 28 result_lines (14 unique signals x duplicate from double-main).
angle_shift_from_r1.rationale: R1's honest-MISS conclusion (CE-3: Montgomery conj. is itself conditional on RH) ruled out the analytical ladder as a proof chain. Round 2 instead asks 'do the numerical hallmarks of RH hold on a finite probe?' — an orthogonal question that can falsify RH numerically (if any probe failed) or add diagnostic signal (if all probes match).

data_source.zeros: Odlyzko first 50 imaginary parts gamma_k of non-trivial zeros of zeta(s)
data_source.precision_used: f64 (~15 digit), embedded literal; no network fetch
data_source.assumption: all rho_k = 1/2 + i gamma_k (RH); values from Odlyzko verified numerically to 10^-15

A_zero_density.label: DERIVED
A_zero_density.verdict: no RH pathology — formula tracks zero count within bounded-O(1) window, matches Riemann-von Mangoldt theorem

B_explicit_formula.label: DERIVED
B_explicit_formula.measurement: K-truncated partial sums converging to the zero-contribution term of psi(6) explicit formula
B_explicit_formula.verdict: partials oscillate around target; no numerical violation. Truncation error O(1/gamma_K) prevents tighter match at K=50.

C_gue_spacing.label: DERIVED
C_gue_spacing.probe: Wigner surmise GUE p(s) = (32/pi^2) s^2 exp(-4s^2/pi) vs Poisson p(s)=exp(-s)
C_gue_spacing.measurement: chi^2 of 10-bin histogram of normalized gaps sn = (gamma_{k+1}-gamma_k)*log(gamma_k/(2pi))/(2pi)
C_gue_spacing.verdict: GUE ~6.9x better fit than Poisson; confirms Montgomery-Odlyzko universality hypothesis on this finite sample

D_li_criterion.label: DERIVED
D_li_criterion.probe: lambda_n = sum_rho (1 - (1 - 1/rho)^n), Li (1997): lambda_n > 0 for all n <=> RH
D_li_criterion.measurement: truncated lambda_n with K=50 zeros, n=1..10
D_li_criterion.verdict: partial positive for n=1..10 — consistent with RH but diagnostic-only (K=50 truncation inadequate for proof-grade test)

hexa_language_gaps: No native complex type — simulated via float pairs inline (avoids project_hexa_struct_return_bug.md ≥3-float corruption)
f64 arithmetic only — no arbitrary precision, K=50 hard ceiling on practical probe size
No erf/zeta/complex-exp standard lib — implemented sin/cos/exp/ln/sqrt via Taylor/Newton series inline
With native complex + GMP bindings we could extend to K=10^4 zeros (Odlyzko's full published table) and reach Li n=30+. Currently blocked by language feature set.

result_summary.jsonl_entries: 14 unique emit() calls x double-main duplication pattern (same as BT-543/BT-545)
verdict_lines: no numerical RH violation detected on first 50 Odlyzko zeros
Li criterion lambda_1..10 all positive as truncated sum
RvM formula matches zero count within O(log T)
result_summary.rh_verdict: 0/1 MISS — round 2 cannot prove RH, and no finite-K numerical attack can by design. RH requires info on all countable-infinity zeros.

honest_verdict_on_hexa_progress: hexa CAN produce diagnostic numerical probes of RH at modest K, matching known universality results. hexa CANNOT produce proof-grade output on any Clay-Millennium problem without (i) native complex arithmetic, (ii) arbitrary precision, (iii) integration with external zero-database. Even with all three, RH is infinite in nature; numerical work is always diagnostic, never proof. This is an intrinsic limitation of computational approaches, not of hexa specifically.

round_3_options.direction: Hilbert-Polya candidate: search n=6 arithmetic for self-adjoint operator patterns
Li lambda_n extended via GMP FFI + Odlyzko full zero table
Random matrix theory deeper bridge — beyond Wigner to Tracy-Widom edge stats on scaled zero imaginary parts
Quantitative de Bruijn-Newman constant Λ (Tao-Rodgers 2020, Λ >= 0) combined with Theorem B n=6 anchor

next_round_candidates.recommended: R3-C (deeper RMT) for next numerical round; R3-D if analytical appetite returns
